overview: DHCP Snooping and Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) are Layer-2 'First Hop Security' features. DHCP Snooping blocks rogue DHCP servers and builds a binding table; DAI uses that same table to block ARP-spoofing / man-in-the-middle attacks.
definitions: Trusted port: a DHCP Snooping port facing the legitimate DHCP server, allowed to send server messages (Offer/Ack).
Untrusted port: a port facing end users; server-only messages arriving here are dropped.
Binding table: the DHCP Snooping database of (MAC, IP, VLAN, Interface) for every legitimate DHCP-leased client.
Option 82: relay information some switches add to DHCP packets; must usually be disabled on switches not acting as DHCP relays.
core_concepts: DHCP Snooping classifies ports Trusted (server side) or Untrusted (user side) and drops DHCPOFFER/DHCPACK arriving on untrusted ports.
DAI compares every ARP message on untrusted ports against the DHCP Snooping binding table; a MAC/IP mismatch means the packet is dropped.
DAI has no independent data source — it is entirely dependent on DHCP Snooping's binding table being populated.
protocol_operation: DHCP Snooping inspects DHCP messages, builds the binding table from legitimate exchanges seen on trusted ports.
DAI intercepts ARP requests/replies on untrusted ports and validates MAC-to-IP mapping against the binding table before forwarding.
packet_flow: Legitimate client DHCP exchange on trusted (server) port -> DHCP Snooping records (MAC, IP, VLAN, Port) in binding table -> later, an ARP packet arrives on an untrusted port -> DAI checks its MAC/IP against the binding table -> match: forwarded; mismatch: dropped (blocks ARP spoofing/MITM).

config_commands.purpose: Globally enable DHCP Snooping on the switch.
Enable DHCP Snooping for a specific VLAN.

config_commands.command: no ip dhcp snooping information option
config_commands.purpose: Disable Option 82 insertion on switches that are not acting as a DHCP relay.
Enable Dynamic ARP Inspection for a VLAN.
Mark an interface as trusted for ARP inspection.
Add an additional DAI check on the Ethernet source MAC vs. the ARP payload MAC.

verification_commands.purpose: Display the DHCP Snooping binding table.
Display DAI status and statistics per VLAN.

show_commands.purpose: Primary verification for the binding table DAI depends on.

config_examples.title: DHCP Snooping + DAI on an access switch
config_examples.code:
ip dhcp snooping
ip dhcp snooping vlan 10
no ip dhcp snooping information option
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 description Link to DHCP server
 ip dhcp snooping trust
 ip arp inspection trust
!
ip arp inspection vlan 10

troubleshooting.symptom: DHCP Snooping just enabled; all users suddenly stop getting IP addresses, even though the DHCP server is healthy and connected on Gi0/1.
troubleshooting.cause: Gi0/1 (the server-facing port) was never marked trusted, so the switch drops the server's own Offer/Ack messages as untrusted.
troubleshooting.fix: Configure 'ip dhcp snooping trust' on Gi0/1.

troubleshooting.symptom: DAI enabled without DHCP Snooping active.
troubleshooting.cause: DAI has no binding table to validate against.
troubleshooting.fix: DAI will not function correctly — DHCP Snooping must be enabled and populated first.

best_practices: Always trust the DHCP server-facing port for both Snooping and DAI in the same pass — forgetting one causes a partial, confusing failure.
Disable Option 82 on switches that are not DHCP relays.
security_notes: Together these two features are the standard defense against rogue DHCP servers and ARP-spoofing based man-in-the-middle attacks on the LAN.
interview_questions: Why would DAI fail immediately after being enabled on a switch that has never run DHCP Snooping?
What exactly gets dropped by an untrusted DHCP Snooping port, and why?
Explain how the binding table connects DHCP Snooping and DAI.
exam_traps: Forgetting to trust the server-facing port after enabling DHCP Snooping — this is the single most common exam and real-world mistake.
Believing DAI can work independently of DHCP Snooping — it cannot.
enterprise_notes: These are typically enabled together as a pair whenever a customer requires Layer-2 hardening on access switches.
